content:
5 min read
Just now

--

Every time you ask a chatbot a question, get a video recommendation, or let your maps app find you a faster route, you’re using artificial intelligence. And that AI is using a surprising amount of electricity.
Behind the scenes, AI runs on huge data centers full of powerful computer chips called GPUs. Each one can use as much electricity as a dishwasher or a stove, and unlike your dishwasher, these chips run nonstop, all day and all night, all year long.
Now compare that to your own brain. It handles many of the same jobs we ask AI to do, like recognizing faces, understanding language, and learning from experience, using roughly a millionth of the energy. Scientists have long dreamed of building computer chips that work more like the brain instead of like today’s power-hungry processors. That field is called neuromorphic engineering, and a recent discovery might have just found a shortcut to get there.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Then Someone Forgot to Plug In a Wire
Our brains are made of neurons and synapses. Neurons are like tiny messengers that send electrical signals, and synapses are the connections between them that can get stronger or weaker over time, which is how we learn.
Trying to copy this in silicon has always been expensive. Engineers have tried building brand new experimental parts, but those tend to be unreliable. More often, they’ve built a single brain-like neuron by wiring together dozens, sometimes hundreds, of ordinary computer parts. That works, but it takes up a lot of space and costs a lot to manufacture. It’s a bit like building a car engine out of a thousand bicycle parts instead of just designing one properly.
That’s what makes a recent discovery so exciting: researchers found that one of the simplest, cheapest parts in all of electronics, the same basic switch found in nearly every computer chip, can act like a neuron and a synapse almost by itself. And they found it completely by accident.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The part in question is called a MOSFET (you can think of it as an electronic on/off switch). It has four connection points, but one of them, called the “bulk” terminal, is usually just hooked up to a ground wire and mostly ignored, since its only real job is to drain away some unwanted extra electrical charge.
One day, a researcher testing a circuit simply forgot to connect that fourth terminal. Instead of the switch behaving normally, something strange happened. The electrical current suddenly spiked upward, then settled back down on its own once the voltage was lowered. That pattern, a quick spike followed by a return to rest, looks a lot like how a real neuron fires and then relaxes.
Here’s the surprising explanation. It turns out every one of these ordinary switches secretly contains a second, hidden type of transistor buried inside it, one that nobody normally pays attention to. During regular use, a small amount of stray electrical charge builds up inside the chip. Normally, that charge safely drains away through the bulk terminal. But when that terminal is left unconnected, the charge has nowhere to go. It builds up and up until it crosses a tipping point, and once it does, this hidden transistor suddenly switches on, causing the current spike. Lower the voltage, and the extra charge fades away, resetting everything, just like a neuron cooling back down after firing a signal.
Once scientists understood what was happening, they figured out how to control it precisely, by carefully adjusting the resistance on that bulk terminal. When they did, every single test switch fired at exactly the same voltage, with almost no variation between them. They also ran the switches through more than 10 million on-off cycles, and not a single one failed.
The Same Chip Was Hiding a Second Trick
The story doesn’t end there. While studying this effect further, the researchers noticed something else. Some of that stray electrical charge doesn’t just drain away, it gets trapped inside a thin layer of the chip near the switch’s control gate. That trapped charge quietly changes how easily electricity flows through the switch from then on, and this change is stable and adjustable.
That’s exactly what a synapse needs to do: hold a connection strength that can be turned up or down and then stay that way. Push one type of charge in, and the connection gets weaker. Push in the other type, and it gets stronger. In other words, the very same cheap, everyday switch, wired slightly differently, can double as a synapse.
Wire a Few Together, and a Tiny Brain Emerges
Imagine wiring up a few of these switches to act as synapses, all feeding into one more switch acting as a neuron. Each “synapse” switch is tuned to a certain strength. Small electrical pulses pass through them and come out scaled stronger or weaker, depending on that tuning. Those signals all arrive at the “neuron” switch, gradually building up charge, and once enough signals arrive close together, the neuron switch fires its own pulse forward. That’s essentially how real brain circuits work too: gathering little signals until they add up to something big enough to act on.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Why does this matter? Because it’s incredibly efficient. Existing brain-inspired chips can already use up to a thousand times less power than regular processors doing the same job, but they typically need dozens or even hundreds of switches to build just one neuron or synapse. This new approach only needs one or two. And because it uses the same basic, cheap manufacturing process that’s already used to make nearly every chip in the world, it wouldn’t require inventing an entirely new production line.
Could This Be the Future of AI Chips?
To be clear, this technology isn’t going to replace the chips in your phone or the servers running AI anytime soon. There’s still a lot of work ahead: refining computer models of how these switches behave, testing full circuits built from many of them, designing the supporting electronics needed to drive them, and running more rounds of manufacturing to fine-tune performance.
The first practical use is likely to be small, battery-powered devices that need a bit of built-in intelligence without draining their battery, think smart sensors or wearables. But if this approach can eventually be scaled up, it raises a fascinating possibility: that one of the cheapest, most ordinary parts in all of electronics could one day help make AI dramatically more energy efficient, using tools we already know how to build.
